---
title: "Lab 04: Data Science Metrics"
---

::: {.lab-question}
**Fundamental question:** Why can accuracy be useless under severe class imbalance?
:::

## Short Intuition

If anomalies are less than `0.1%` of all events, a classifier can be almost perfectly accurate by predicting "normal" every time. Precision and recall force us to ask better questions: how many flagged cases were real, and how many real anomalies did we catch?

## Executable Code Block

```python
import torch

torch.manual_seed(17)

n = 10_000
y_true = torch.zeros(n, dtype=torch.int64)
y_true[torch.randperm(n)[:8]] = 1

scores = 0.04 * torch.randn(n)
scores[y_true == 1] += 0.18
y_pred = (scores > 0.10).to(torch.int64)

tp = ((y_true == 1) & (y_pred == 1)).sum()
fp = ((y_true == 0) & (y_pred == 1)).sum()
fn = ((y_true == 1) & (y_pred == 0)).sum()
tn = ((y_true == 0) & (y_pred == 0)).sum()

precision = tp / (tp + fp).clamp_min(1)
recall = tp / (tp + fn).clamp_min(1)
beta = 2.0
f_beta = (1 + beta**2) * precision * recall / ((beta**2 * precision + recall).clamp_min(1e-12))
accuracy = (tp + tn) / n

print({"tp": int(tp), "fp": int(fp), "fn": int(fn), "tn": int(tn)})
print("accuracy:", accuracy.item())
print("precision:", precision.item())
print("recall:", recall.item())
print("F2:", f_beta.item())
```

## Hands-on Tasks & Solutions

1. Lower the threshold from `0.10` to `0.06`. What happens to precision and recall?
2. Explain why `F2` is more appropriate than `F1` when missing anomalies is expensive.

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

Lowering the threshold usually increases recall because more true anomalies are flagged. It also usually decreases precision because more normal observations become false positives.

`F2` weights recall four times as strongly as precision because `beta^2 = 4`. That is useful when investigation cost is acceptable but missed anomalies are dangerous.

```python
beta = 2.0
f2 = (1 + beta**2) * precision * recall / ((beta**2 * precision + recall).clamp_min(1e-12))
```

</details>
